title: Check if a path exists between u and v.
description: Check if a path exists between u and v.

examples.explanation: Start at node 0. Mark 0 as visited. Check neighbors: 1 and 2. First explore neighbor 1: Mark 1 visited. Check neighbors of 1: [2]. Explore neighbor 2: Mark 2 visited. Check neighbors of 2: [0, 3]. 0 is visited, skip. Explore neighbor 3: Mark 3 visited. 3 equals v, return true. Backtrack: Unmark 3, return true to 2, return true to 1, return true to 0. Path found: 0->1->2->3.

Start at node 0. Mark 0 visited. Check neighbors: [1]. Explore neighbor 1: Mark 1 visited. Check neighbors of 1: [2]. Explore neighbor 2: Mark 2 visited. Check neighbors of 2: [1]. 1 is visited, skip. No more neighbors, return false. Backtrack: Unmark 2, return false to 1. No more neighbors for 1, return false to 0. No path to 3 found.

approaches.name: Brute Force: Enumerate All Paths
approaches.summary: Generate all possible paths from u to v by exploring all possible sequences of intermediate nodes. This works for small graphs but becomes impractical quickly due to combinatorial explosion.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Path Exists Between Two Nodes Using Brute Force Path Enumeration
ladders.desc: Given a graph and two nodes u and v, determine if there exists any path from u to v by exploring all possible sequences of intermediate nodes.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the path existence problem by systematically exploring every possible route from the starting node u to the target node v. The intuition is straightforward: if a path exists, we'll eventually find it by trying all possibilities, but this comes at the cost of exponential time complexity. The algorithm uses depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the graph, keeping track of visited nodes to avoid cycles. At each node, it recursively explores all unvisited neighbors, continuing until either v is found or all possibilities are exhausted. The worst-case time complexity is O(n!) for a complete graph with n nodes because we might explore all permutations of nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and visited set. This approach connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive exploration logic. Common mistakes include not handling cycles properly (leading to infinite recursion), forgetting to mark nodes as unvisited during backtracking, and inefficiently checking all neighbors when early termination is possible. The real code pattern involves a recursive helper function that explores each neighbor, marks nodes as visited, and returns true if the target is found.

examples.trace: Start at node 0. Mark 0 as visited. Check neighbors: 1 and 2. First explore neighbor 1: Mark 1 visited. Check neighbors of 1: [2]. Explore neighbor 2: Mark 2 visited. Check neighbors of 2: [0, 3]. 0 is visited, skip. Explore neighbor 3: Mark 3 visited. 3 equals v, return true. Backtrack: Unmark 3, return true to 2, return true to 1, return true to 0. Path found: 0->1->2->3.

Start at node 0. Mark 0 visited. Check neighbors: [1]. Explore neighbor 1: Mark 1 visited. Check neighbors of 1: [2]. Explore neighbor 2: Mark 2 visited. Check neighbors of 2: [1]. 1 is visited, skip. No more neighbors, return false. Backtrack: Unmark 2, return false to 1. No more neighbors for 1, return false to 0. No path to 3 found.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS to Explore All Paths from a Node
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that explores all possible paths from a current node to find a target node, avoiding cycles.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for traversing the graph recursively to find if a path exists from the current node to the target. The intuition is to treat each node as a decision point: from the current node, we try every unvisited neighbor, diving deeper until we either find the target or exhaust all options. The algorithm uses DFS: mark the current node as visited, check if it's the target, then recursively call itself on each unvisited neighbor. If any recursive call returns true, we propagate true back; otherwise, we backtrack by unmarking the node and return false. Time complexity is O(n!) in worst case due to exploring all permutations; space is O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. This feeds into L0 by providing the path exploration logic. Common mistakes include not unmarking nodes during backtracking (which prevents exploring alternative paths), not checking for cycles (leading to infinite loops), and not handling base cases correctly. The real code pattern is a recursive function with parameters for current node, target, visited set, and graph adjacency list.

examples.trace: Call with current=0, target=3. Mark 0 visited. 0 != 3. Explore neighbors: [1, 2]. First neighbor 1: Recursive call with current=1. Mark 1 visited. 1 != 3. Explore neighbors: [3]. Neighbor 3: Recursive call with current=3. Mark 3 visited. 3 == 3, return true. Backtrack: Unmark 3, return true to 1, return true to 0. Path found: 0->1->3.

Call with current=0, target=2. Mark 0 visited. 0 != 2. Explore neighbors: [1]. Neighbor 1: Recursive call with current=1. Mark 1 visited. 1 != 2. Explore neighbors: [0]. 0 is visited, skip. No more neighbors, return false. Backtrack: Unmark 1, return false to 0. No more neighbors for 0, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with Visited Set to Avoid Cycles
ladders.desc: Use a visited set to track explored nodes and backtrack by unmarking nodes, enabling exploration of all paths without getting stuck in cycles.
ladders.explanation: This core logic ensures that while exploring all possible paths, we avoid infinite loops by tracking visited nodes and properly backtracking. The intuition is that to enumerate all paths, we need to explore each node multiple times in different contexts, but not revisit the same node within a single path to prevent cycles. The algorithm involves marking a node as visited when we enter it, exploring its neighbors, and then unmarking it when we leave (backtrack), allowing it to be part of other paths. This is key for brute force enumeration. Time complexity contributes to the overall O(n!) by allowing full permutation exploration; space is O(n) for the visited set. This feeds into L1 by providing the cycle avoidance mechanism. Common mistakes include forgetting to unmark nodes (which blocks alternative paths), using a global visited set without backtracking, or incorrectly managing the visited state across recursive calls. The real code pattern is to add the current node to a visited set, recurse, then remove it after recursion.

examples.trace: Start at 0. Add 0 to visited. Explore neighbors: [1, 2]. Try neighbor 1: Add 1 to visited. Explore neighbors of 1: [0]. 0 is visited, skip. No path to 2 from 1, remove 1 from visited, backtrack. Try neighbor 2: Add 2 to visited. 2 == target, return true. Remove 2 from visited, return true.

Start at 0. Add 0 to visited. Explore neighbors: [1]. Try neighbor 1: Add 1 to visited. Explore neighbors of 1: [0]. 0 is visited, skip. No path to 2, remove 1 from visited, backtrack. No more neighbors, remove 0 from visited, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Using Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Traverse a graph represented as an adjacency list, visiting each neighbor of a node sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through the neighbors of a node in a graph, which is fundamental for exploring paths. The intuition is that to find paths between nodes, we need to move from one node to its connected nodes, and an adjacency list provides an efficient way to access these connections. The algorithm is simple: given a node, access its list of neighbors from the adjacency list, and process each neighbor one by one. This is a basic step in graph algorithms. Time complexity is O(degree of node) per access; space is O(1) for the iteration. This feeds into L2 by enabling the exploration of neighbors during backtracking. Common mistakes include assuming the graph is directed or undirected incorrectly, not handling missing nodes in the adjacency list, or iterating over neighbors in a way that modifies the list during traversal. The real code pattern is a for-loop over graph[node] to access each neighbor.

examples.trace: Access graph[0] which returns [1, 2]. Iterate: first neighbor is 1, then neighbor 2. Process completes.
Access graph[1] which returns [2]. Iterate: neighbor is 2. Process completes.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check if a Node is in a Set
ladders.desc: Determine whether a given node is present in a set of visited nodes, a fundamental operation for cycle detection.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves checking membership in a set, which is crucial for avoiding cycles during graph traversal. The intuition is that to prevent revisiting the same node and getting stuck in a loop, we need a fast way to test if a node has already been explored in the current path. Sets provide O(1) average-time membership checks, making them ideal for this purpose. The algorithm is straightforward: given a node and a set, use the 'in' operator to return true if the node is in the set, false otherwise. This operation is performed repeatedly during traversal. Time complexity is O(1) on average; space is O(1) for the check. This feeds into L3 by enabling the decision to skip visited neighbors during iteration. Common mistakes include using a list instead of a set (leading to O(n) checks), not initializing the set properly, or modifying the set while checking membership. The real code pattern is 'if node in visited_set: ...'.

examples.trace: Check if 3 is in {1, 2, 3}. Since 3 is present, return true.
Check if 0 is in {1, 2}. Since 0 is not present, return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Representing a Graph as an Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Understand how a graph is represented using an adjacency list, where each node maps to a list of its neighboring nodes.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the adjacency list representation of a graph, which is essential for the brute force path enumeration approach. The intuition is that a graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them, and an adjacency list provides a simple way to store these connections: for each node, we maintain a list of nodes it is directly connected to. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs and allows quick access to neighbors. For example, in an undirected graph, if node A is connected to node B, then B appears in A's list and A appears in B's list. The algorithm involves creating a dictionary or list where keys are node identifiers and values are lists of neighbor identifiers. Time complexity for accessing neighbors is O(1) on average; space is O(V + E) for V vertices and E edges. This feeds into L4 by providing the data structure for neighbor checks. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected graphs (e.g., not adding reciprocal edges), using incorrect data types, or not handling nodes with no neighbors. The real code pattern is a dictionary like graph = {0: [1, 2], 1: [0], 2: [0]}.

examples.trace: For edge (0,1): add 1 to list of 0, add 0 to list of 1. For edge (0,2): add 2 to list of 0, add 0 to list of 2. For edge (1,2): add 2 to list of 1, add 1 to list of 2. Result: 0 connects to 1 and 2; 1 connects to 0 and 2; 2 connects to 0 and 1.
For directed edge (0,1): add 1 to list of 0. For directed edge (1,2): add 2 to list of 1. Node 2 has no outgoing edges, so empty list. Result: 0->1, 1->2.

approaches.name: Graph Traversal: BFS/DFS
approaches.summary: Use standard graph traversal algorithms (BFS or DFS) starting from node u to search for node v. This is the standard approach for path existence problems in graphs.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(V + E) where V is vertices, E is edges
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V) for visited set and queue/stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Path Existence via Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BFS/DFS to check if a path exists between two nodes in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete path existence problem solved using graph traversal. The intuition is that if nodes u and v are connected, we can start from u and systematically explore reachable nodes until we either find v or exhaust all possibilities. BFS explores level by level using a queue, while DFS goes deep first using recursion or a stack. Both algorithms maintain a visited set to avoid cycles and infinite loops. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list from edges. 2) Initialize visited set and queue/stack with u. 3) While queue/stack not empty: pop node, if node == v return True, else mark visited and add unvisited neighbors. 4) Return False if loop ends. Time is O(V+E) as each node/edge processed once. Space is O(V) for visited and queue/stack. Connection to L1: This integrates the traversal subroutine with graph representation. Common mistakes: forgetting visited set (causes infinite loops), incorrect adjacency list building, not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def has_path(edges, u, v): graph = build_adj_list(edges); visited = set(); queue = deque([u]); while queue: node = queue.popleft(); if node == v: return True; visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: queue.append(neighbor); return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: Initialize visited={}, queue=[0]. Step 3: Pop 0, not v, visited={0}, add neighbor 1 → queue=[1]. Step 4: Pop 1, not v, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 2 → queue=[2]. Step 5: Pop 2, not v, visited={0,1,2}, add neighbor 3 → queue=[3]. Step 6: Pop 3, equals v → return True.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: Initialize visited={}, queue=[0]. Step 3: Pop 0, not v, visited={0}, add neighbor 1 → queue=[1]. Step 4: Pop 1, not v, visited={0,1}, no new neighbors → queue=[]. Step 5: Queue empty → return False (3 unreachable from 0).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Graph Traversal (BFS/DFS) from Source
ladders.desc: Implement BFS or DFS traversal starting from a source node to explore reachable nodes.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs graph traversal from a source node, which is the core engine for path checking. The intuition is to systematically visit nodes reachable from the source without repetition. BFS uses a queue for level-order exploration, ensuring shortest path in unweighted graphs; DFS uses a stack/recursion for depth-first exploration. The algorithm: 1) Initialize visited set and queue/stack with source. 2) While queue/stack not empty: pop node, mark visited, add unvisited neighbors. 3) Continue until all reachable nodes visited. Time O(V+E), space O(V). Connection to L0: This traversal is embedded in the full solution to search for target v. Common mistakes: not marking visited before adding to queue (causes duplicates), incorrect neighbor iteration. Real code pattern: def traverse(graph, source): visited = set(); queue = deque([source]); while queue: node = queue.popleft(); if node not in visited: visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: queue.append(neighbor); return visited.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited={}, queue=[0]. Step 2: Pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbors 1,2 → queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 3 → queue=[2,3]. Step 4: Pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, no new neighbors → queue=[3]. Step 5: Pop 3, visited={0,1,2,3}, no new neighbors → queue=[].

Step 1: visited={}, queue=[2]. Step 2: Pop 2, visited={2}, add neighbor 1 → queue=[1]. Step 3: Pop 1, visited={2,1}, add neighbor 0 → queue=[0]. Step 4: Pop 0, visited={2,1,0}, no new neighbors → queue=[].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Visited Set Management for Cycle Prevention
ladders.desc: Maintain a visited set to track explored nodes and avoid infinite loops in graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the visited set, which is essential for correct graph traversal. The intuition is that graphs can have cycles, so revisiting nodes causes infinite loops; the visited set prevents this by marking nodes as explored. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty set. 2) When visiting a node, add it to the set. 3) Before processing a neighbor, check if it's in the set to skip already visited nodes. Time O(1) per check, space O(V). Connection to L1: The visited set is integrated into the traversal subroutine. Common mistakes: adding to visited after popping (allows duplicates), not checking visited before enqueueing. Real code pattern: visited = set(); visited.add(node); if neighbor not in visited: process neighbor.

examples.input: Traverse graph with cycle: 0->1->2->0, start at 0
examples.trace: Step 1: visited={}, queue=[0]. Step 2: Pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbor 1 → queue=[1]. Step 3: Pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 2 → queue=[2]. Step 4: Pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, neighbor 0 is in visited → skip, queue=[].

Step 1: visited={}, queue=[0]. Step 2: Pop 0, visited={0}, add neighbor 1 → queue=[1]. Step 3: Pop 1, visited={0,1}, add neighbor 2 → queue=[2]. Step 4: Pop 2, visited={0,1,2}, no neighbors → queue=[].

ladders.title: Building Block — Adjacency List Representation
ladders.desc: Convert edge list to adjacency list for efficient graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block creates an adjacency list from a list of edges, which is the standard graph representation for traversal algorithms. The intuition is that adjacency lists allow O(1) access to neighbors, making traversal efficient. The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty dictionary. 2) For each edge [a,b]: add b to a's list and a to b's list (for undirected graph). 3) Ensure all nodes have entries (even if empty). Time O(E), space O(V+E). Connection to L2: The adjacency list feeds into traversal where visited set manages nodes from this list. Common mistakes: forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, not handling missing nodes. Real code pattern: graph = defaultdict(list); for a,b in edges: graph[a].append(b); graph[b].append(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: graph={}. Step 2: Edge [0,1] → graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0]. Step 3: Edge [1,2] → graph[1]=[0,2], graph[2]=[1]. Step 4: Edge [2,3] → graph[2]=[1,3], graph[3]=[2].
Step 1: graph={}. Step 2: Edge [0,1] → graph[0]=[1], graph[1]=[0]. Step 3: Edge [0,2] → graph[0]=[1,2], graph[2]=[0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue/Stack Operations for BFS/DFS
ladders.desc: Implement fundamental queue (FIFO) or stack (LIFO) operations used in traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers queue and stack data structures, which are essential for BFS and DFS respectively. The intuition is that queues process nodes in the order they're discovered (level-order), while stacks process the most recent node first (depth-first). For queue: enqueue (append) to back, dequeue (popleft) from front. For stack: push (append) to top, pop (pop) from top. Time O(1) per operation, space O(V). Connection to L3: These operations manage node order in traversal using the adjacency list. Common mistakes: using wrong structure (queue vs stack), not checking empty before pop. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(1); node = queue.popleft(); stack = []; stack.append(1); node = stack.pop().

examples.input: Queue operations: start empty, enqueue 0,1,2, dequeue twice
examples.trace: Step 1: queue=[]. Step 2: enqueue 0 → queue=[0]. Step 3: enqueue 1 → queue=[0,1]. Step 4: enqueue 2 → queue=[0,1,2]. Step 5: dequeue → returns 0, queue=[1,2]. Step 6: dequeue → returns 1, queue=[2].

examples.input: Stack operations: start empty, push 0,1,2, pop twice
examples.trace: Step 1: stack=[]. Step 2: push 0 → stack=[0]. Step 3: push 1 → stack=[0,1]. Step 4: push 2 → stack=[0,1,2]. Step 5: pop → returns 2, stack=[0,1]. Step 6: pop → returns 1, stack=[0].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Node and Edge Basics
ladders.desc: Understand fundamental graph concepts: nodes (vertices) and edges (connections).
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces the basic elements of a graph. A graph consists of nodes (vertices) representing entities and edges representing connections between them. In an undirected graph, edges are bidirectional (e.g., if node A connects to B, then B connects to A). The intuition is that graphs model relationships, like social networks or road maps. For path existence, we care whether a sequence of edges links two nodes. Example: nodes {0,1,2}, edges {(0,1),(1,2)} means 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 2, so path exists from 0 to 2 via 0-1-2. Time/space not applicable here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: Nodes are stored in queues/stacks during traversal. Common mistakes: confusing directed/undirected edges, not identifying all nodes. Real code pattern: nodes are integers or objects; edges are pairs like (0,1).

examples.output: Description: 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 2, 2 connected to 1, 0 not directly connected to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: List nodes: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge (0,1) connects 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge (1,2) connects 1 and 2. Step 4: Check connections: 0's neighbors: {1}; 1's neighbors: {0,2}; 2's neighbors: {1}.

examples.output: Description: A connected to B and C, B connected to A and C, C connected to B and A
examples.trace: Step 1: List nodes: A,B,C. Step 2: Edge (A,B) connects A and B. Step 3: Edge (B,C) connects B and C. Step 4: Edge (C,A) connects C and A. Step 5: Check connections: A's neighbors: {B,C}; B's neighbors: {A,C}; C's neighbors: {B,A}.

testCases.expected: 0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 0 and 2, 2 connected to 1
A connected to B, B connected to A
1 connected to 2, 2 connected to 1 and 3, 3 connected to 2
X connected to Y and Z, Y connected to X and Z, Z connected to Y and X
0 connected to 1, 1 connected to 0, 2 connected to 3, 3 connected to 2
A connected to B, B connected to A and C, C connected to B and D, D connected to C
0 connected to 1 and 2, 1 connected to 0, 2 connected to 0

approaches.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
approaches.summary: Preprocess the graph using union-find data structure to connect components, then check if u and v are in the same connected component. This is efficient for multiple queries on the same graph.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(E * α(V)) for preprocessing, O(α(V)) per query
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(V) for parent and rank arrays

ladders.title: Full Problem — Path Existence Check Using Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution to check if a path exists between two nodes using Union-Find preprocessing
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the problem of checking connectivity between nodes u and v in an undirected graph. The Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) approach is particularly efficient when we have multiple queries on the same static graph. The intuition is to preprocess the graph by connecting all nodes that are reachable from each other into the same connected components using union operations. Once the components are established, checking if u and v are connected becomes a simple find operation to see if they belong to the same component. The algorithm works by initializing each node as its own parent (representative of its component). We iterate through all edges, performing union operations on the endpoints to merge their components. After preprocessing, each query involves finding the root representatives of u and v and checking if they are equal. The time complexity is O(E * α(V)) for preprocessing (where α is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant) and O(α(V)) per query, with space O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This approach is optimal for multiple queries because it amortizes the preprocessing cost. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression or union by rank properly, which can lead to worse time complexity, or misunderstanding that this works only for undirected graphs. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the union and find operations implemented in the key sub-routine.

examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3,4], rank = [0,0,0,0,0]. Process edge (0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Process edge (1,2): find(1)=0 (after path compression), find(2)=2, union(0,2): parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. Process edge (3,4): find(3)=3, find(4)=4, union(3,4): parent[4]=3, rank[3]=1. Now components: {0,1,2} and {3,4}. Query (0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=0 (after path compression), same component → True. Query (0,4): find(0)=0, find(4)=3, different components → False.

Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0]. Process edge (0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1): parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Process edge (2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, union(2,3): parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. Process edge (1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union(0,2): since rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, choose 0 as root, parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Now all nodes in one component. Query (0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=0 (after path compression: find(3) goes to 2 then 0), same component → True. Query (1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=0, same component → True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Union and Find Operations
ladders.desc: Implement the union and find operations with path compression and union by rank
ladders.explanation: The union and find operations are the core of the Union-Find data structure. Find operation determines the representative (root) of a node's component, while union merges two components. The intuition is to maintain a forest of trees where each tree represents a connected component, with the root as the representative. Find uses recursion or iteration to traverse parent pointers until reaching the root, and path compression flattens the tree by making nodes point directly to the root during the traversal, improving future queries. Union by rank ensures trees remain balanced by attaching the shorter tree under the root of the taller tree, preventing degenerate chains. The algorithm: initialize parent[i]=i and rank[i]=0. Find(x): if parent[x]!=x, set parent[x]=find(parent[x]) (path compression), return parent[x]. Union(x,y): find roots rootX and rootY; if different, compare ranks: if rank[rootX]<rank[rootY], set parent[rootX]=rootY; else if rank[rootX]>rank[rootY], set parent[rootY]=rootX; else set parent[rootY]=rootX and increment rank[rootX]. Time complexity is O(α(n)) per operation due to optimizations, space O(n). This sub-routine feeds into L0 by enabling efficient component management. Common mistakes include forgetting path compression or rank updates, leading to O(n) time. Real code pattern: class UnionFind with __init__, find, union methods.

examples.input: Initialize with 4 nodes, perform union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2)
examples.output: Final parents: [0,0,0,0] or similar with ranks updated
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, set parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, set parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, set parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Final: parent=[0,0,0,0], rank=[2,0,1,0].

examples.input: Initialize with 5 nodes, perform union(0,1), union(2,3), union(3,4), find(4)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): set parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): set parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. union(3,4): find(3)=2, find(4)=4, set parent[4]=2, rank[2]=1. find(4): parent[4]=2, parent[2]=2, return 2, and compress path: set parent[4]=2 directly.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Component Connectivity via Root Comparison
ladders.desc: Determine if two nodes are connected by checking if they have the same root representative
ladders.explanation: The central insight of Union-Find is that two nodes are in the same connected component if and only if they share the same root representative in the disjoint set forest. After preprocessing the graph with union operations, the data structure partitions nodes into sets where each set has a unique root. To check connectivity between u and v, we simply perform find(u) and find(v) and compare the results. If they are equal, a path exists; otherwise, no path exists. This works because union operations merge components by making their roots point to a common root, effectively recording reachability. The intuition is that the root acts as a 'label' for the component, and all nodes in that component will have that label after path compression. The algorithm is straightforward: after building the Union-Find structure with all edges, for each query (u,v), return find(u) == find(v). Time complexity is O(α(V)) per query, space O(V). This logic is used in L1 as the final step in union operations and feeds into L0 for answering queries. Common mistakes include not updating the structure after new edges (if graph is dynamic) or assuming it works for directed graphs. Real code pattern: if uf.find(u) == uf.find(v): return True else False.

examples.input: Components: {0,1,2} with root 0, {3,4} with root 3, query (0,2)
examples.trace: find(0) returns 0, find(2) returns 0 (after path compression), 0 == 0 → True.

examples.input: Components: {0,1} with root 0, {2,3} with root 2, query (0,3)
examples.trace: find(0) returns 0, find(3) returns 2, 0 != 2 → False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Edge Processing
ladders.desc: Represent the graph and iterate through edges to perform union operations
ladders.explanation: To apply Union-Find, we need to represent the graph and process its edges to build connected components. The graph is typically given as a list of edges or an adjacency list. For Union-Find, we only need the list of edges since it's an undirected graph; each edge (u,v) indicates that u and v are directly connected and should be in the same component. The intuition is that by taking the transitive closure of these direct connections via union operations, we implicitly capture all paths in the graph. The algorithm: initialize a UnionFind structure with V nodes. For each edge (u,v) in the edge list, call union(u,v). This iteratively merges components until all nodes reachable via any path are in the same set. Time complexity is O(E * α(V)) for processing all edges, space O(V) for the UnionFind structure plus O(E) for storing edges if needed. This building block feeds into L2 by creating the component structure used for root comparisons. Common mistakes include processing edges in the wrong order (order doesn't matter for undirected graphs) or missing edges. Real code pattern: for u,v in edges: uf.union(u,v).

examples.output: After processing, all nodes in one component
examples.trace: Initialize UnionFind with 4 nodes. Process (0,1): union(0,1) merges {0} and {1}. Process (2,3): union(2,3) merges {2} and {3}. Process (1,2): union(1,2) merges {0,1} and {2,3} into {0,1,2,3}.
Initialize UnionFind with 5 nodes. Process (0,1): union(0,1) merges {0} and {1}. Process (3,4): union(3,4) merges {3} and {4}. Nodes 2 remains in its own component.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Parent Array Initialization and Updates
ladders.desc: Initialize and maintain the parent array that tracks each node's parent in the Union-Find tree
ladders.explanation: The parent array is a fundamental data structure in Union-Find that represents the forest of trees. Each index corresponds to a node, and the value at that index is the parent of the node in the tree. Initially, each node is its own parent, meaning it is the root of its own component (parent[i] = i). As union operations merge components, we update parent pointers to reflect the new tree structure. For example, when unioning two nodes, we set the parent of one root to point to the other root. The intuition is that this array encodes the connectivity: by following parent pointers from any node, we can find its root representative. The algorithm involves: initialization of an array of size V with parent[i] = i. During union, after finding roots, update parent[rootY] = rootX or vice versa. During find, we traverse parent pointers recursively. Time complexity for updates is O(1), but traversal can be O(log V) without optimizations. Space is O(V). This operation feeds into L3 as part of building the Union-Find structure. Common mistakes include not initializing properly or updating the wrong index. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)).

examples.input: Initialize for 3 nodes, then set parent[1] = 0 to union(0,1)
examples.trace: Start: parent = [0,1,2]. After union(0,1): find roots: 0 and 1, set parent[1] = 0. Result: parent = [0,0,2].

examples.input: Initialize for 4 nodes, union(0,1), union(2,3), union(1,2)
examples.trace: Start: parent = [0,1,2,3]. union(0,1): set parent[1]=0 → [0,0,2,3]. union(2,3): set parent[3]=2 → [0,0,2,2]. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, set parent[2]=0 → [0,0,0,2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets and Connectivity in Graphs
ladders.desc: Understand the basic idea of connected components and set membership
ladders.explanation: At the foundation, we need to grasp what it means for two nodes in a graph to be connected. In an undirected graph, a path exists between nodes u and v if there is a sequence of edges leading from u to v. This defines an equivalence relation: nodes are in the same connected component if they are reachable from each other. The intuition is that the graph can be partitioned into disjoint sets (connected components), where each set contains nodes that are all mutually reachable. For example, in a social network, people in the same friend group form a connected component. The basic operation is to determine set membership: given two nodes, are they in the same set? This is a fundamental problem in graph theory. The algorithm conceptually involves grouping nodes into sets based on edges. Time and space depend on implementation, but the idea is simple. This concept feeds into L4 as we use parent arrays to represent these sets. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs or not considering transitive connectivity. Real code pattern: think of components as lists of nodes, e.g., components = [{0,1}, {2,3}].

examples.trace: Node 0 and 1 are connected by an edge, so they are in the same set. Node 2 has no edges to others, so it is in its own set.
Edge (0,1) connects 0 and 1 into one set. Edge (2,3) connects 2 and 3 into another set. No edges between these sets, so they are separate.
